comments:
Summary:
The submission describes a hierarchical, vision-only approach to translate short videos into Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) triplets. The system uses pre-trained image CNN features (fc7) as visual cues, performs unsupervised selection of top features and key frames to form foreground descriptors (L1-FG), augments these with background/scene descriptors to form level-2 classifiers (L2-FG-BG), and finally composes contextual co-occurrence and coarse temporal-region based features into level-3 classifiers (L3 and L3-Temp). Linear SVMs are used at all stages. The method is evaluated on the Chen & Dolan YouTube video dataset and is reported to give substantial relative improvements in semantic (WUP) similarity and to outperform several prior methods (including some LSTM-based approaches).

Strengths:
- The paper presents a clear, intuitively motivated pipeline that exploits foreground/background separation and contextual co-occurrence between subjects, verbs and objects. The multi-level hierarchy is well explained.
- The unsupervised feature/frame selection idea is simple and efficient; reported relative gains (selection of top 60 features and top 50 frames) indicate the approach materially improves performance versus naive pooling.
- Emphasis on exploiting visual context alone (without external language corpora) is an interesting and relevant research angle.
- The method is computationally simple (fc7 + linear SVMs) compared to complex sequence models, which is a practical advantage.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- The provided text makes claims about outperforming competing models and provides some relative percentage improvements, but the excerpt does not include absolute numerical results, full tables, or statistical significance. Reproducibility would benefit from clearer, complete quantitative results in the paper (per-class scores, baselines, confidence intervals).
- Relying solely on static-image CNN features to predict verbs is a risky design choice for verbs that are inherently motion-dependent. The paper acknowledges this but should provide a more detailed analysis of which verb classes benefit and which fail (failure cases).
- It is not fully clear how robust the unsupervised foreground selection is across highly cluttered videos or in the presence of camera motion; more diagnostics or visualizations of selected frames/features and ablation studies would strengthen the argument.
- While the multi-level contextual idea is valuable, some of its parts are heuristic (choice of k=60, q=50, c for top responses, three temporal segments). The paper should provide sensitivity analyses or guidelines for these hyperparameters and discuss computational costs.
- The related-work discussion is comprehensive, but stronger controlled comparisons (including motion-based baselines, and latest video feature models) would be useful to justify claims of state-of-the-art performance.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Include full quantitative tables in the main text (not only referenced), with per-class breakdowns and ablation experiments that isolate the contribution of L1, L2, and L3, as well as the temporal segmentation.
- Add experiments that include motion features (optical flow, 3D ConvNet) or at least compare to such baselines to demonstrate when static fc7 features suffice and when motion is necessary.
- Provide qualitative examples of failure cases and visualizations of the chosen foreground frames/features to help readers understand strengths and limitations.
- Discuss generalization: how does the method scale to larger vocabularies or more complex sentences beyond atomic SVO triplets?
- Consider releasing code and trained models to facilitate reproducibility.

Verdict:
This is a well-motivated and clearly presented work that contributes a simple, effective hierarchical visual approach for SVO extraction from video. Given the novelty of using contextual co-occurrence and unsupervised foreground selection in a compact pipeline, together with the promising reported improvements, I recommend acceptance (favoring an oral presentation). The main area to strengthen is in providing fuller quantitative evidence, ablations, and analyses of limitations.
